The dataset is loaded from an Excel file using read_excel() from the readxl package. Four derived time variables are added for temporal analysis.

superstore <- read_excel("data/Superstore.xls")

# Add derived time variables for temporal analysis
superstore <- superstore %>%
  mutate(
    Year      = year(`Order Date`),
    Month     = month(`Order Date`, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE),
    YearMonth = floor_date(`Order Date`, "month"),
    Quarter   = paste0("Q", quarter(`Order Date`)),
    ShipDays  = as.numeric(difftime(`Ship Date`, `Order Date`, units = "days"))
  )

cat(sprintf("✅ Dataset loaded: %d rows × %d columns\n",
            nrow(superstore), ncol(superstore) - 5))

✅ Dataset loaded: 10194 rows × 21 columns

cat(sprintf("📅 Date range: %s  →  %s\n",
            format(min(superstore$`Order Date`), "%d %b %Y"),
            format(max(superstore$`Order Date`), "%d %b %Y")))

📅 Date range: 03 Jan 2022  →  30 Dec 2025

cat(sprintf("🗂  Categories: %s\n",
            paste(unique(superstore$Category), collapse = " | ")))

🗂  Categories: Office Supplies | Furniture | Technology

cat(sprintf("🌍  Regions: %s\n",
            paste(unique(superstore$Region), collapse = " | ")))

🌍  Regions: Central | East | South | West

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.1 By Category


Show Code
category_metrics <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Profit   = sum(Profit),
    Total_Sales    = sum(Sales),
    Avg_Profit     = mean(Profit),
    Median_Profit  = median(Profit),
    Profit_Margin  = Total_Profit / Total_Sales * 100,
    Count          = n(),
    Pct_Profitable = mean(Profit > 0) * 100,
    .groups        = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total_Profit))

# Panel A: Boxplot profit distribution
p_cat_box <- ggplot(superstore,
                    aes(x = reorder(Category, Profit, FUN = median),
                        y = Profit, fill = Category)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.75, outlier.alpha = 0.25, outlier.size = 1,
               outlier.color = "grey60") +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 23, size = 4.5,
               fill = "white", color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e74c3c", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Profit Distribution by Category",
    subtitle = "Diamond = mean  |  Box = IQR  |  Whiskers = 1.5×IQR  |  Red dashed = break-even",
    x = NULL, y = "Profit per Transaction"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"), legend.position = "none")

# Panel B: Total profit bar
p_cat_total <- ggplot(category_metrics,
                      aes(x = reorder(Category, Total_Profit),
                          y = Total_Profit, fill = Category)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(Total_Profit, accuracy = 1)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 4.2, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels  = dollar_format(),
                     expand  = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.28))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Total Profit by Category", x = NULL, y = "Total Profit") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

# Panel C: Margin vs % Profitable bubble
p_cat_bubble <- ggplot(category_metrics,
                       aes(x = Profit_Margin, y = Pct_Profitable,
                           color = Category, size = Total_Sales,
                           label = Category)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(size = 4, fontface = "bold", show.legend = FALSE,
                  box.padding = 0.5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_size_continuous(labels = dollar_format(), name = "Total Sales",
                        range = c(8, 18)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 1), "%")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 1), "%"),
                     limits = c(50, 100)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Profit Margin vs % Profitable Transactions",
    subtitle = "Bubble size ∝ Total Sales",
    x = "Overall Profit Margin (%)",
    y = "% of Transactions with Profit > 0"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"), legend.position = "none")

(p_cat_box | p_cat_total) / p_cat_bubble















Tip



Findings:

Technology leads with $146,543 in total profit and a 17.4% profit margin, despite having fewer transactions than Office Supplies.
The bubble chart reveals that higher margin does not always mean higher total profit — transaction volume matters.
Furniture shows the lowest % of profitable transactions, indicating a systemic pricing or discounting problem in that category.

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.2 By Sub-Category

Lollipop ChartTreemap




Show Code
subcategory_metrics <- superstore %>%
  group_by(`Sub-Category`, Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Total_Sales  = sum(Sales),
    Avg_Profit   = mean(Profit),
    Count        = n(),
    .groups      = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(Total_Profit)

# Colour flag
subcategory_metrics <- subcategory_metrics %>%
  mutate(Profitable = Total_Profit > 0)

ggplot(subcategory_metrics,
       aes(x = reorder(`Sub-Category`, Total_Profit),
           y = Total_Profit, color = Profitable)) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = reorder(`Sub-Category`, Total_Profit), yend = 0),
               linewidth = 1.3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 5.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = dollar(Total_Profit, accuracy = 1),
        hjust = ifelse(Total_Profit >= 0, -0.18, 1.18)),
    size = 3.0, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#27ae60", "FALSE" = "#e74c3c"),
                     labels  = c("FALSE" = "Loss-making", "TRUE" = "Profitable"),
                     name    = "Status") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0.2, 0.2))) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_grid(Category ~ ., scales = "free_y", space = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title    = "Total Profit by Sub-Category",
    subtitle = "Grouped by main Category  |  Red = loss-making sub-categories",
    x = NULL, y = "Total Profit",
    caption  = "Source: Superstore Dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11,
                                   color = "white",
                                   margin = margin(4, 4, 4, 4)),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#2c3e50", color = NA),
    legend.position  = "bottom"
  )













Show Code
subcategory_tm <- subcategory_metrics %>%
  mutate(
    abs_profit = abs(Total_Profit),
    tile_label = paste0(`Sub-Category`, "\n",
                        dollar(Total_Profit, accuracy = 1))
  )

ggplot(subcategory_tm,
       aes(area      = abs_profit,
           fill      = Total_Profit,
           label     = tile_label,
           subgroup  = Category)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(color = "white", linewidth = 3) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_text(
    place = "topleft", grow = FALSE, reflow = TRUE,
    colour = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 14, alpha = 0.65
  ) +
  geom_treemap_text(
    color = "white", place = "centre",
    reflow = TRUE, size = 9, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = "#e74c3c",
    mid      = "#f5f5f5",
    high     = "#27ae60",
    midpoint = 0,
    labels   = dollar_format(),
    name     = "Total Profit"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Treemap: Sub-Category Profitability",
    subtitle = "Tile size ∝ |Profit|   |   Colour: Green = Profitable · Red = Loss",
    caption  = "Source: Superstore Dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
    legend.position = "right"
  )


















Warning



Critical Finding:
3 sub-categories are generating net losses: Tables (-$17,753.21), Bookcases (-$3,632.07), Supplies (-$1,171.39). The worst is Tables at –$17,753.21. These require immediate strategic review — consider repricing, capping discounts, or discontinuation.

title: In-class Exercise
text:
6.1 Sales & Profit Trends


Show Code
monthly_by_cat <- superstore %>%
  group_by(YearMonth, Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales  = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    .groups      = "drop"
  )

monthly_overall <- superstore %>%
  group_by(YearMonth) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales   = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit  = sum(Profit),
    Profit_Margin = Total_Profit / Total_Sales * 100,
    .groups       = "drop"
  )

# Sales trend by category
p_sales_trend <- ggplot(monthly_by_cat,
                        aes(x = YearMonth, y = Total_Sales, color = Category)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_point(size = 1.6, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  labs(title = "Monthly Sales Trend by Category",
       x = NULL, y = "Total Sales", color = "Category") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Profit trend by category
p_profit_trend <- ggplot(monthly_by_cat,
                         aes(x = YearMonth, y = Total_Profit, color = Category)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  labs(title = "Monthly Profit Trend by Category",
       x = NULL, y = "Total Profit", color = "Category") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Profit margin area + LOESS
p_margin_trend <- ggplot(monthly_overall,
                          aes(x = YearMonth, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_area(fill = "#3498db", alpha = 0.18) +
  geom_line(color = "#2c3e50", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "#e74c3c",
              fill = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.15, linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "#e74c3c", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 1), "%")) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  labs(
    title    = "Monthly Overall Profit Margin",
    subtitle = "Red LOESS line = long-run trend  |  Shaded band = 95% CI",
    x = NULL, y = "Profit Margin (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

p_sales_trend / p_profit_trend / p_margin_trend

title: In-class Exercise
text:
6.2 Seasonal Patterns


Show Code
heatmap_data <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Year, Month) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Total_Sales  = sum(Sales),
    .groups      = "drop"
  )

# Month × Year profit heatmap
p_heatmap <- ggplot(heatmap_data,
                    aes(x = Month, y = factor(Year), fill = Total_Profit)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = dollar(Total_Profit, accuracy = 0.1, scale = 0.001, suffix = "K")),
    size = 3.2, fontface = "bold", color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#e74c3c", mid = "grey88", high = "#27ae60",
    midpoint = 0, name = "Total Profit",
    labels = dollar_format()
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Profit Heatmap — Year × Month",
    subtitle = "Green = high profit  |  Red = low or negative profit",
    x = "Month", y = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )

# Avg monthly sales ridge across years
monthly_avg <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Month, Category) %>%
  summarise(Avg_Sales = mean(Sales), .groups = "drop")

p_season <- ggplot(monthly_avg,
                   aes(x = Month, y = Avg_Sales, fill = Category)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.85, alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  labs(
    title    = "Average Monthly Sales by Category",
    subtitle = "Seasonal peaks are most visible in Q4 (Oct–Dec)",
    x = "Month", y = "Avg Sales per Month",
    fill = "Category"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

p_season / p_heatmap















Tip



Temporal Findings:

Dec is consistently the strongest month, averaging $11,057.46 in profit — likely driven by end-of-year purchasing.
Jan is the weakest, averaging $2,374.31.
The LOESS trend in the margin chart reveals the long-run health trajectory of the business.

title: In-class Exercise
text:
6.3 Interactive Time Series (Plotly)


Show Code
p_anim <- plot_ly(
  monthly_by_cat,
  x      = ~YearMonth,
  y      = ~Total_Profit,
  color  = ~Category,
  colors = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(3, "Set1"),
  type   = "scatter",
  mode   = "lines+markers",
  line   = list(width = 2),
  marker = list(size = 5),
  text   = ~paste0(
    "<b>", Category, "</b><br>",
    format(YearMonth, "%b %Y"), "<br>",
    "Profit: ", dollar(Total_Profit, accuracy = 1)
  ),
  hovertemplate = "%{text}<extra></extra>"
) %>%
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "<b>Monthly Profit by Category (Interactive)</b><br><sup>Drag slider to zoom — hover for details</sup>"
    ),
    xaxis = list(
      title       = "",
      rangeslider = list(visible = TRUE),
      rangeselector = list(
        buttons = list(
          list(count = 6,  label = "6M",  step = "month", stepmode = "backward"),
          list(count = 12, label = "1Y",  step = "month", stepmode = "backward"),
          list(count = 24, label = "2Y",  step = "month", stepmode = "backward"),
          list(step = "all", label = "All")
        )
      )
    ),
    yaxis       = list(title = "Total Profit ($)", tickformat = "$,.0f"),
    legend      = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = -0.25),
    hovermode   = "x unified",
    plot_bgcolor  = "rgba(0,0,0,0)",
    paper_bgcolor = "rgba(0,0,0,0)"
  )

title: In-class Exercise
text:
8.1 Performance Summary


Show Code
# Compute all metrics dynamically — no hardcoding
# Compute all metrics dynamically — no hardcoding
pct_loss_txns <- mean(superstore$Profit < 0)

summary_metrics <- tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Total Transactions",
    "Total Sales Revenue",
    "Total Profit",
    "Overall Profit Margin",
    "Unprofitable Transactions",
    "Total Loss (Unprofitable Orders)",
    "Most Profitable Category",
    "Least Profitable Sub-Category",
    "Best Region (Total Profit)",
    "Best Region (Profit Margin)",
    "Avg Discount — Profitable Orders",
    "Avg Discount — Unprofitable Orders"
  ),
  Value = c(
    format(nrow(superstore), big.mark = ","),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Sales)),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit)),
    paste0(round(sum(superstore$Profit) / sum(superstore$Sales) * 100, 2), " %"),
    paste0(format(sum(superstore$Profit < 0), big.mark = ","),
           "  (", round(pct_loss_txns * 100, 1), " % of orders)"),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit[superstore$Profit < 0])),
    superstore %>% group_by(Category) %>%
      summarise(tp = sum(Profit), .groups = "drop") %>%
      slice_max(tp, n = 1) %>% pull(Category),
    superstore %>% group_by(`Sub-Category`) %>%
      summarise(tp = sum(Profit), .groups = "drop") %>%
      slice_min(tp, n = 1) %>% pull(`Sub-Category`),
    superstore %>% group_by(Region) %>%
      summarise(tp = sum(Profit), .groups = "drop") %>%
      slice_max(tp, n = 1) %>% pull(Region),
    superstore %>% group_by(Region) %>%
      summarise(ts = sum(Sales), tp = sum(Profit), .groups = "drop") %>%
      mutate(margin = tp / ts) %>%
      slice_max(margin, n = 1) %>% pull(Region),
    percent(mean(superstore$Discount[superstore$Profit > 0]), accuracy = 0.1),
    percent(mean(superstore$Discount[superstore$Profit <= 0]), accuracy = 0.1)
  )
)

title: In-class Exercise
text:
8.2 Interactive Profitability Dashboard


Show Code
fig_cat <- plot_ly(
  category_metrics,
  x    = ~reorder(Category, Total_Profit),
  y    = ~Total_Profit,
  type = "bar",
  color = ~Category,
  colors = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(3, "Set2"),
  text = ~paste0(
    "<b>", Category, "</b><br>",
    "Total Profit: ", dollar(Total_Profit), "<br>",
    "Profit Margin: ", round(Profit_Margin, 1), "%<br>",
    "Transactions: ", format(Count, big.mark = ",")
  ),
  hovertemplate = "%{text}<extra></extra>"
) %>%
  layout(
    title   = "<b>Category</b>",
    xaxis   = list(title = ""),
    yaxis   = list(title = "Total Profit ($)", tickformat = "$,.0f"),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )

fig_reg <- plot_ly(
  regional_metrics,
  x    = ~reorder(Region, Total_Profit),
  y    = ~Total_Profit,
  type = "bar",
  color = ~Region,
  colors = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(4, "Set3"),
  text = ~paste0(
    "<b>", Region, "</b><br>",
    "Total Profit: ", dollar(Total_Profit), "<br>",
    "Profit Margin: ", round(Profit_Margin, 1), "%<br>",
    "Transactions: ", format(Count, big.mark = ",")
  ),
  hovertemplate = "%{text}<extra></extra>"
) %>%
  layout(
    title   = "<b>Region</b>",
    xaxis   = list(title = ""),
    yaxis   = list(title = "Total Profit ($)", tickformat = "$,.0f"),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )

fig_seg <- plot_ly(
  segment_metrics,
  x    = ~reorder(Segment, Avg_Profit),
  y    = ~Avg_Profit,
  type = "bar",
  color = ~Segment,
  colors = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(3, "Dark2"),
  text = ~paste0(
    "<b>", Segment, "</b><br>",
    "Avg Profit: ", dollar(Avg_Profit, accuracy = 0.01), "<br>",
    "Total Profit: ", dollar(Total_Profit), "<br>",
    "% Profitable: ", round(Pct_Profitable, 1), "%"
  ),
  hovertemplate = "%{text}<extra></extra>"
) %>%
  layout(
    title   = "<b>Customer Segment</b>",
    xaxis   = list(title = ""),
    yaxis   = list(title = "Avg Profit ($)", tickformat = "$,.2f"),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )

subplot(fig_cat, fig_reg, fig_seg, nrows = 1,
        titleX = TRUE, titleY = TRUE, shareY = FALSE,
        margin = 0.06) %>%
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "<b>Superstore Profitability Dashboard</b><br><sup>Hover over bars for details</sup>",
      x    = 0.5
    ),
    plot_bgcolor  = "rgba(0,0,0,0)",
    paper_bgcolor = "rgba(0,0,0,0)"
  )












Note📋 Business Recommendations



1. Discount Strategy Reform

Implement a hard discount cap at 30 % — data shows that losses spike sharply beyond this threshold, especially in Furniture.
Profitable orders average 8.0% discount; unprofitable average 46.9% — closing this gap is the single highest-leverage action available.
Require management sign-off for any discount exceeding 20 %.

2. Product Line Optimisation

Loss-making sub-categories requiring action: Tables (-$17,753.21) · Bookcases (-$3,632.07) · Supplies (-$1,171.39).
Consider repricing or discontinuing Tables (worst performer).
Double down on: Copiers, Phones, Accessories — the top-3 most profitable sub-categories.

3. Regional Strategy

West region: proven performance — maintain investment and use as the benchmark.
Central region: diagnose root causes — is it discount abuse, product mix, or market conditions? Implement corrective actions in the next quarter.

4. Segment Development

Home Office: highest profit per transaction — grow this segment through targeted outreach and premium product bundles.
Consumer: high volume, lower margin — focus campaigns on reducing discount frequency rather than acquiring more customers.
Corporate: offer value-added services, SLAs, and dedicated account management instead of price discounts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text: This hands-on exercise explores how to bring data stories to life through animation. We move beyond static charts to create animated and interactive visualisations using gganimate and plotly — tools that let data evolve over time and invite audience engagement.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
1.1 Overview
When telling a visually-driven data story, animated graphics tends to attract the interest of the audience and make deeper impressions than static graphics. In this hands-on exercise, we learn how to create animated data visualisations by using gganimate and plotly R packages. We also learn how to:

Reshape data using the tidyr package
Process, wrangle and transform data using the dplyr package


1.1.1 Basic Concepts of Animation
When creating animations, the plot does not actually move. Instead, many individual plots are built and then stitched together as movie frames — just like an old-school flip book or cartoon. Each frame is a different plot when conveying motion, which is built using some relevant subset of the aggregate data. The subset drives the flow of the animation when stitched back together.



Animation concept: individual frames stitched together




1.1.2 Terminology
Before diving into creating animated statistical graphs, it is important to understand the key concepts:

Frame: Each frame represents a different point in time or a different category. When the frame changes, the data points on the graph are updated to reflect the new data.
Animation Attributes: Settings that control how the animation behaves — such as the duration of each frame, the easing function used to transition between frames, and whether to start from the current frame or from the beginning.







TipWhen Should You Animate?



Before making animated graphs, ask: Does it make sense to go through the effort?

For exploratory data analysis: an animated graphic may not be worth the time investment.
For presentations: a few well-placed animated graphics can help an audience connect with your topic remarkably better than static counterparts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
1.2 Getting Started

1.2.1 Loading the R Packages
The following R packages are used in this exercise:







Package
Purpose




readxl
Import Excel workbooks


gifski
Convert video frames to GIF animations with efficient cross-frame palettes


gapminder
Provides the country_colors colour scheme


plotly
Interactive statistical graphs with animation support


gganimate
Extend ggplot2 grammar to include animation


tidyverse
Data wrangling, transformation and static visualisation





Show code
pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse, ggplot2, dplyr)
options(gganimate.renderer = magick_renderer())








NoteWhat I Learned



Using pacman::p_load() is a cleaner alternative to repeated library() calls — it installs missing packages automatically and loads all listed packages in one line. This keeps the setup code concise and reproducible across different machines.





1.2.2 Importing the Data
The Data worksheet from the GlobalPopulation Excel workbook is used throughout this exercise. The data captures population statistics (percentage aged, percentage young, total population) across countries and continents over multiple years.
Evolution of the import code — from deprecated to modern dplyr:
The original code used deprecated functions. The modern equivalent using across() is preferred:


Show code
col <- c("Country", "Continent")

globalPop <- read_xls("data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet = "Data") %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(col), as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))








NoteWhat I Learned




read_xls() from readxl handles Excel files natively without needing to save as CSV first.
The shift from mutate_each_() + funs() → mutate_at() → mutate(across()) reflects how the dplyr API matured toward a more consistent, expressive design. across() is the modern standard and works seamlessly inside any mutate() or summarise() call.
Converting Year to integer (not double) is critical for transition_time() to treat it correctly as a discrete time variable.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
1.3 Data Exploration
Before animating, it is essential to understand the structure and distribution of the data. Visual exploration helps us identify patterns, outliers and whether animation will add analytical value.

1.3.1 Dataset Structure


Show code
glimpse(globalPop)


Rows: 6,204
Columns: 6
$ Country    <fct> "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan",…
$ Year       <int> 1996, 1998, 2000, 2002, 2004, 2006, 2008, 2010, 2012, 2014,…
$ Young      <dbl> 83.6, 84.1, 84.6, 85.1, 84.5, 84.3, 84.1, 83.7, 82.9, 82.1,…
$ Old        <dbl> 4.5, 4.5, 4.5, 4.5, 4.5, 4.6, 4.6, 4.6, 4.6, 4.7, 4.7, 4.7,…
$ Population <dbl> 21559.9, 22912.8, 23898.2, 25268.4, 28513.7, 31057.0, 32738…
$ Continent  <fct> Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia,…




Show code
summary(globalPop)


        Country          Year          Young             Old       
 Afghanistan:  28   Min.   :1996   Min.   : 15.50   Min.   : 1.00  
 Albania    :  28   1st Qu.:2010   1st Qu.: 25.70   1st Qu.: 6.90  
 Algeria    :  28   Median :2024   Median : 34.30   Median :12.80  
 Andorra    :  28   Mean   :2023   Mean   : 41.66   Mean   :17.93  
 Angola     :  28   3rd Qu.:2038   3rd Qu.: 53.60   3rd Qu.:25.90  
 Anguilla   :  28   Max.   :2050   Max.   :109.20   Max.   :77.10  
 (Other)    :6036                                                  
   Population                Continent   
 Min.   :      3.3   Africa       :1568  
 1st Qu.:    605.9   Asia         :1454  
 Median :   5771.6   Europe       :1344  
 Mean   :  34860.9   North America: 976  
 3rd Qu.:  22711.0   Oceania      : 526  
 Max.   :1807878.6   South America: 336  
                                         




1.3.2 Distribution of Key Variables


Show code
p1 <- ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#3182bd", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of % Aged Population",
       x = "% Aged", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p2 <- ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Young)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#e6550d", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of % Young Population",
       x = "% Young", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p3 <- ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Population / 1e6)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#31a354", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Population Size",
       x = "Population (Millions)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

library(patchwork)
p1 | p2 | p3





Distribution of % Aged, % Young, and Population across all years










NoteWhat I Learned



The histograms immediately reveal that population size is highly right-skewed — a few very populous countries (e.g. China, India) dwarf the rest. This skew is why bubble charts use scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) to compress the visual range and prevent tiny countries from becoming invisible.




1.3.3 Trend Over Time by Continent


Show code
globalPop %>%
  group_by(Continent, Year) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean_Old   = mean(Old,   na.rm = TRUE),
    Mean_Young = mean(Young, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Mean_Old, Mean_Young),
               names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value") %>%
  mutate(Metric = recode(Metric,
                         "Mean_Old"   = "% Aged",
                         "Mean_Young" = "% Young")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Value, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ Metric, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Demographic Shift by Continent Over Time",
       subtitle = "Mean % Aged rises as % Young falls — the global ageing trend",
       x = "Year", y = "Mean Percentage",
       colour = "Continent") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")





Mean % Aged and % Young by Continent across Years










NoteWhat I Learned



This faceted line chart confirms the demographic transition narrative that the animated bubble chart will later tell dynamically. Ageing populations rise consistently across all continents while the proportion of young people declines — animation will make this drift visually compelling in ways a static chart cannot.




1.3.4 Old vs Young: Scatter by Continent (Static Snapshot)


Show code
ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Old vs Young: All Countries, All Years",
       subtitle = "Strong negative correlation — as ageing rises, youth proportion falls",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young",
       colour = "Continent") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")





Old vs Young % coloured by Continent — all years combined










NoteWhat I Learned



The clear negative correlation between % Aged and % Young across all data points (all countries, all years pooled) gives us strong motivation to animate: by splitting this cloud of points by Year, the animation will reveal how the entire distribution shifts toward higher ageing over decades — something impossible to perceive in a single static chart.




1.3.5 Number of Countries per Continent


Show code
globalPop %>%
  distinct(Country, Continent) %>%
  count(Continent, name = "n_countries") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Continent, n_countries), y = n_countries,
             fill = Continent)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n_countries), hjust = -0.2, size = 4) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Dataset Coverage: Countries per Continent",
       x = "Continent", y = "Number of Countries") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  ylim(0, 60)





Number of countries per continent in the dataset










NoteWhat I Learned



Africa and Asia each have many countries in the dataset while Oceania has few. This imbalance is worth noting when interpreting the animated bubble plot — the bubbles for Africa will be densely clustered while Oceania may appear sparse. country_colors from gapminder ensures consistent colouring regardless of this count difference.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
1.4 Animated Data Visualisation: gganimate Methods
gganimate extends ggplot2’s grammar of graphics to include animation. It does so through new grammar classes added to the plot object:



Function family
Role




transition_*()
How data is spread across time


view_*()
How positional scales change during animation


shadow_*()
How data from other time points is shown


enter_*()/exit_*()
How new data appears / old data disappears


ease_aes()
Controls the pacing of aesthetic transitions




1.4.1 Building a Static Population Bubble Plot
Before animating, we first build the static version. This helps verify the visual design and aesthetics before adding motion.


Show code
ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}",
       subtitle = "Static version — all years overlaid",
       x = "% Aged", 
       y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)





Static bubble plot — all years overlaid (note the {frame_time} placeholder in title)










NoteWhat I Learned



The static plot with all years overlaid creates visual noise — overlapping bubbles make it impossible to distinguish temporal change. The {frame_time} placeholder in the title does not yet render because there is no transition_time() applied. This clearly shows why animation adds value: without it, the temporal narrative is completely hidden.
scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) is critical — it maps the raw population values (which span orders of magnitude) to a visually sensible dot size range so small countries remain visible.





1.4.2 Building the Animated Bubble Plot
Now we add the gganimate layer. transition_time(Year) slices the data by Year and renders one frame per year, creating the impression of temporal movement.


Show code
ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}",
       subtitle = "Each bubble = one country. Size = Population.",
       x = "% Aged", 
       y = "% Young") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)





Animated bubble chart using gganimate — countries shift rightward (ageing) over time










NoteWhat I Learned




transition_time(Year) is the core animation function. It automatically creates one frame per unique Year value and interpolates bubble positions between frames using the easing function.
ease_aes("linear") makes each aesthetic change at a constant rate across the transition. Alternative easing methods (quadratic, elastic, bounce, etc.) create different “feel” — linear is most honest for showing demographic data where we want precise temporal mapping.
The animated GIF makes the rightward drift (increasing % Aged) and downward drift (decreasing % Young) immediately obvious — the global demographic transition becomes a visual narrative rather than a table of numbers.
show.legend = FALSE works correctly in gganimate (unlike in plotly, as we will see).




1.4.2.1 Deeper Exploration: Easing Function Comparison
To understand the impact of different easing functions, we compare linear vs cubic-in-out:


Show code
library(gifski)

anim_linear <- ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young,
                                      size = Population,
                                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Easing: linear | Year: {frame_time}",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

# Render to a temporary file
gif_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".gif")
animate(anim_linear, nframes = 60, fps = 8,   # reduced frames to avoid gifski errors
        width = 600, height = 400,
        renderer = gifski_renderer(gif_file))

# Include the GIF
knitr::include_graphics(gif_file)




Show code
library(gifski)

anim_cubic <- ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young,
                                     size = Population,
                                     colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Easing: cubic-in-out | Year: {frame_time}",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("cubic-in-out") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

gif_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".gif")
animate(anim_cubic, nframes = 60, fps = 8,
        width = 600, height = 400,
        renderer = gifski_renderer(gif_file))

knitr::include_graphics(gif_file)








NoteWhat I Learned



The easing function controls the perceived rhythm of animation. linear gives equal visual weight to each time step — analytically the most truthful option for time-series data. cubic-in-out creates a slow → fast → slow motion that feels more polished for presentations but can misrepresent the rate of demographic change between years. Choosing easing is both a design and integrity decision.




1.4.2.2 Deeper Exploration: shadow_wake() for Trajectory Trails
shadow_wake() leaves a fading “wake” behind each bubble, making individual country trajectories visible even during the animation.


Show code
ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}  |  Trails show historical trajectory",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear") +
  shadow_wake(wake_length = 0.2, alpha = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)





Adding shadow_wake() to reveal country trajectories during animation










NoteWhat I Learned



shadow_wake() is a powerful addition when you want to show where a data point has been, not just where it is now. The wake_length parameter controls how much historical trail is shown (0 = none, 1 = full history). This is especially useful for demographic bubbles because it reveals which countries are ageing rapidly (long rightward trails) versus those that are more stable — analytical insight that would otherwise require a separate panel.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
1.5 Animated Data Visualisation: plotly
plotly offers a different approach to animation: instead of rendering individual frames into a GIF, it creates interactive HTML animations. Both ggplotly() and plot_ly() support key-frame animations through the frame argument/aesthetic. They also support an ids argument/aesthetic to ensure smooth transitions between objects with the same id — facilitating object constancy (a key principle of good animation design).

1.5.1 Building an Animated Bubble Plot: ggplotly() Method

1.5.1.1 First Attempt — Legend Issue
ggplotly() converts a ggplot2 object into an interactive plotly widget. Note the known issue with show.legend = FALSE:


Show code
gg <- ggplot(globalPop, 
             aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                 size = Population, 
                 colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = Population,
                 frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = "% Aged", y = "% Young")

ggplotly(gg)




ggplotly animation — note legend still appears despite show.legend = FALSE








NoteWhat I Learned



show.legend = FALSE inside geom_point() does not suppress the legend in ggplotly(). This is because ggplotly() translates each colour mapping to a separate plotly trace, and trace visibility is controlled separately from ggplot2’s legend logic. The fix is to use ggplot2’s theme(legend.position = 'none') at the plot level — which plotly does respect during conversion.
This is a good reminder that ggplotly() is a translation layer, not a perfect mirror. Some ggplot2 arguments behave differently once converted.




1.5.1.2 Fixed Version — Using theme(legend.position = 'none')


Show code
library(plotly)

gg <- ggplot(globalPop, 
             aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                 size = Population, 
                 colour = Country,
                 frame = Year)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = "% Aged", y = "% Young")

ggplotly(gg) %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE)




ggplotly animation — legend correctly suppressed using theme()








NoteWhat I Learned



The animated bubble plot rendered by ggplotly() includes a play/pause button and a year slider — two interactive controls that the gganimate GIF cannot provide. This interactivity fundamentally changes the viewer experience:

The play/pause allows a presenter to stop at a specific year to discuss it in detail.
The slider allows viewers to manually scrub through time — enabling personalised exploration.
Hover tooltips show exact values for each country’s bubble on demand.

This is the key trade-off: gganimate produces a self-running GIF ideal for embedding in reports or social media, while plotly produces an interactive widget ideal for web dashboards and exploratory analysis.






1.5.2 Building an Animated Bubble Plot: plot_ly() Method
plot_ly() provides more direct control over plotly’s native API. Here we colour by Continent rather than Country, making continental patterns easier to distinguish.


Show code
bp <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x          = ~Old, 
          y          = ~Young, 
          size       = ~Population, 
          color      = ~Continent,
          sizes      = c(2, 100),
          frame      = ~Year, 
          text       = ~Country, 
          hoverinfo  = "text",
          type       = "scatter",
          mode       = "markers") %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE,
         xaxis = list(title = "% Aged"),
         yaxis = list(title = "% Young"),
         title = list(text = "Global Demographics Over Time"))
bp




plot_ly() animated bubble chart — coloured by Continent, hover shows country name








NoteWhat I Learned



plot_ly() gives finer control than ggplotly() for several reasons:

sizes = c(2, 100) maps the population range to plotly’s native size scale (different units from ggplot2’s scale_size()).
hoverinfo = "text" + text = ~Country gives clean, custom tooltips showing only the country name.
color = ~Continent (continent-level colouring) is analytically superior to country-level colouring for spotting continental clusters — Africa’s young/low-aged cluster vs Europe’s aged/low-young cluster become immediately clear.
frame = ~Year is the native plotly equivalent of transition_time(Year) in gganimate — but plotly renders this as a JavaScript-driven interactive animation, not a pre-rendered GIF.




1.5.2.1 Deeper Exploration: plot_ly() with ids for Object Constancy
Adding ids = ~Country ensures that each bubble maintains identity across frames, enabling smooth interpolation (object constancy):


Show code
bp_ids <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x          = ~Old, 
          y          = ~Young, 
          size       = ~Population, 
          color      = ~Continent,
          sizes      = c(2, 100),
          frame      = ~Year,
          ids        = ~Country,
          text       = ~Country, 
          hoverinfo  = "text",
          type       = "scatter",
          mode       = "markers") %>%
  layout(showlegend = TRUE,
         xaxis      = list(title = "% Aged"),
         yaxis      = list(title = "% Young"),
         title      = list(text = "Global Demographics — Smooth Transitions (ids = Country)"),
         legend     = list(title = list(text = "Continent")))
bp_ids




plot_ly() with ids for smooth object constancy across animation frames








NoteWhat I Learned



Object constancy is a fundamental principle of animated visualisation design: viewers should be able to follow a specific object as it moves across frames. Without ids, plotly may re-match bubbles arbitrarily between frames, causing confusing “teleportation” of bubbles. Adding ids = ~Country tells plotly which bubble in frame N corresponds to which bubble in frame N+1, enabling smooth, continuous motion for each country. This makes individual country trajectories trackable by eye — a critical advantage for analytical storytelling.




1.5.2.2 Deeper Exploration: Animated Bubble Chart with Population Labels for Key Countries
For an enhanced presentation-ready version, we highlight the most populous countries with labels:


Show code
# Identify top 5 countries by max population for labelling
top_countries <- globalPop %>%
  group_by(Country) %>%
  summarise(max_pop = max(Population, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  slice_max(max_pop, n = 5) %>%
  pull(Country)

globalPop_enhanced <- globalPop %>%
  mutate(Label = if_else(Country %in% top_countries, as.character(Country), ""))

bp_enhanced <- globalPop_enhanced %>%
  plot_ly(x          = ~Old, 
          y          = ~Young, 
          size       = ~Population, 
          color      = ~Continent,
          sizes      = c(2, 100),
          frame      = ~Year,
          ids        = ~Country,
          text       = ~paste0("<b>", Country, "</b><br>",
                               "Population: ", scales::comma(Population), "<br>",
                               "% Aged: ", round(Old, 1), "%<br>",
                               "% Young: ", round(Young, 1), "%"),
          hoverinfo  = "text",
          type       = "scatter",
          mode       = "markers") %>%
  layout(showlegend = TRUE,
         xaxis      = list(title = "% Aged Population"),
         yaxis      = list(title = "% Young Population"),
         title      = list(text = "Global Demographic Transition Over Time"),
         legend     = list(title = list(text = "Continent")))
bp_enhanced




Enhanced plot_ly() — top 5 most populous countries labelled for tracking








NoteWhat I Learned



Combining ids with rich hover text (paste0() for multi-line tooltips) creates a presentation-quality chart. The custom tooltip reveals Population, % Aged, and % Young simultaneously on hover — providing detailed information without cluttering the visual. This illustrates the key analytical advantage of interactive plotly animations over static charts: viewers can dive deeper into specific data points of interest without the analyst needing to pre-decide what to highlight.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
1.6 Comparison: gganimate vs plotly

gganimate Summaryplotly SummarySide-by-Side Design Decision Guide


Best for:

Embedding animated GIFs in static reports, PDFs, or presentations
Self-running animations in social media or email
Full ggplot2 integration (all geoms, themes, scales work natively)
Offline use

Key functions used:

transition_time() — temporal animation
ease_aes() — controls transition pacing
shadow_wake() — trajectory trails



Best for:

Interactive web dashboards
Exploratory analysis where viewers want to pause, hover and explore
Play/pause + slider controls for presentation
Richer tooltip information on demand

Key functions used:

ggplotly() — converts ggplot2 objects (watch for legend quirks)
plot_ly() — native plotly API with more control
frame = ~Year — keyframe animation
ids = ~Country — object constancy for smooth transitions











Criterion
gganimate
plotly




Output format
GIF / video
Interactive HTML widget


Viewer interactivity
None (auto-plays)
Play/pause, slider, hover


ggplot2 compatibility
Full
Partial (via ggplotly())


Object constancy
Built-in via transition_time()
Requires ids = argument


Best context
Reports, slides, social
Dashboards, web, Shiny


File size
Small GIF
Larger HTML












NoteOverall What I Learned



The most important insight from this exercise is that animation is not just decoration — it is a narrative tool. The demographic transition (ageing populations, declining youth proportions) is a well-documented phenomenon, but seeing it unfold year by year through an animated bubble chart makes it viscerally real in a way that a data table or even a static scatter plot cannot.
The choice between gganimate and plotly is a choice between two kinds of stories: - gganimate tells a directed story — you show the audience what you want them to see, at the pace you choose. - plotly invites exploratory storytelling — the audience can ask their own questions by hovering, pausing and scrubbing through time.
Both are valuable. The key skill is knowing which one serves your audience and context best.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Welcome! This hands-on exercise covers three complementary tools for interactive visualisation in R:

ggiraph — extends ggplot2 with hover tooltips, data_id linking, and onclick actions
plotly — full-featured interactive charts via plot_ly() and ggplotly()
crosstalk — cross-widget interactions linking plots to interactive tables

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
2.1 Installing Packages
The code chunk below uses pacman::p_load() to check, install and launch all required packages in one step.

pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, DT, tidyverse, readxl,
               knitr, patchwork, viridis, scales, crosstalk)

Packages used:

ggiraph — makes ggplot2 graphics interactive via tooltip, data_id, and onclick aesthetics
plotly — creates interactive web graphics from ggplot2 or via its own plot_ly() interface
DT — wraps the JavaScript DataTables library for interactive HTML tables
tidyverse — modern R packages for data science (includes ggplot2, dplyr, readr)
patchwork — combines multiple ggplot2 graphs; used inside girafe() for coordinated views
crosstalk — enables cross-widget interactions (linked brushing and filtering) between htmlwidgets
readxl, viridis, scales — supporting utilities for data import and visual styling

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
2.2 Importing Data

pop_data <- read_xls("data/GlobalPopulation.xls", sheet = "Data") %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor))

The dataset GlobalPopulation.xls contains country-level demographic records with fields: Country, Continent, Year, Population, Young (young-age population), and Old (elderly population). It is the dataset we will use throughout this exercise to demonstrate interactive visualisation techniques.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
4.1 Tooltip Effect with tooltip Aesthetic
The code chunk below plots a basic interactive chart using geom_point_interactive(). Two steps are involved: first build a ggplot object with an interactive geom; then pass it to girafe() to generate an SVG displayed on the HTML page.


Show code
p <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Young, y = Population)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = Country),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7, color = "#831843"
  ) +
  labs(title = "Young vs Population — Basic Tooltip (hover for country name)",
       x = "Young Population", y = "Total Population") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618
)






Interactivity: hovering the mouse over any data point displays the country name. Notice that the only change from a standard ggplot is replacing geom_point() with geom_point_interactive() and adding tooltip = Country inside aes(), then wrapping the whole object in girafe().






NoteWhat I Learned



The two-step pattern — interactive geom → girafe() — is the mental model for all ggiraph work. The SVG output embeds directly in HTML reports with no extra server required. This is the cleanest entry point: minimal code change, immediate hover feedback.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
4.2 Displaying Multiple Information on Tooltip
Tooltip content can be customised by constructing a character column that combines multiple fields:


Show code
latest <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(tooltip_text = paste0(
    "Country = ", Country,
    "\nContinent = ", Continent
  ))

p <- ggplot(data = latest, aes(x = Young, y = Population)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_text),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7, color = "#1e40af"
  ) +
  labs(title = "Young vs Population — Multi-field Tooltip",
       x = "Young Population", y = "Total Population") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618
)






Interactivity: hovering shows both Country and Continent. The paste0() call builds a multi-line string by inserting \n between fields; this string is pre-computed as a new column and then referenced in aes(tooltip = ...). The key insight is that the tooltip column must exist in the data frame before being passed to the aesthetic.






NoteWhat I Learned



Pre-computing a tooltip column with mutate() before the ggplot call keeps the aes() clean and makes complex tooltips easy to debug. Calling paste0() with explicit field labels (e.g., "Country = ") makes tooltips self-explanatory for end users.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
4.3 Customising Tooltip Style
opts_tooltip() inside girafe() applies CSS to the tooltip container:


Show code
tooltip_css <- "background-color:#1e293b; color:#f8fafc; font-weight:bold;
                padding:8px 12px; border-radius:6px; font-size:13px;"

latest <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(tooltip_text = paste0(
    "Country = ", Country,
    "\nContinent = ", Continent
  ))

p <- ggplot(data = latest, aes(x = Young, y = Population)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_text),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7, color = "#059669"
  ) +
  labs(title = "Young vs Population — Styled Tooltip (dark background)",
       x = "Young Population", y = "Total Population") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618,
  options    = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)






The tooltip_css string is a standard CSS declaration. opts_tooltip(css = ...) passes it directly to the SVG tooltip element. The same CSS properties available in web development — background-color, color, font-weight, border-radius — work here. Refer to Customizing girafe objects for a full list of options.






NoteWhat I Learned



Tooltip CSS is applied at the girafe() level, not inside aes(), which keeps styling concerns cleanly separated from data mapping. Dark backgrounds with light text (#1e293b / #f8fafc) immediately distinguish the tooltip from the chart background.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
4.4 Displaying Statistics on Tooltip
A function can compute derived statistics and embed them in the tooltip using after_stat():


Show code
tooltip_fn <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = 0.01) {
  mean_val <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem_val  <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean Young Population:", mean_val, "+/-", sem_val)
}

gg_bar <- ggplot(data = pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)),
                 aes(x = Continent)) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(y = Young,
        tooltip = after_stat(tooltip_fn(y, ymax))),
    fun.data = "mean_se",
    geom     = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill     = "lightblue"
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(y = Young),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom     = "errorbar",
    width    = 0.2, linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  labs(title = "Mean Young Population by Continent — Statistics on Tooltip",
       x = "Continent", y = "Mean Young Population") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))

girafe(ggobj = gg_bar, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10 * 0.618)






after_stat() defers evaluation of tooltip_fn(y, ymax) until stat_summary has computed the summary statistics, meaning y and ymax refer to the computed mean and upper confidence bound — not raw data columns. GeomInteractiveCol is the interactive version of GeomCol, enabling the tooltip on bar charts.






NoteWhat I Learned



after_stat() is the bridge between statistical transformations and interactive aesthetics. Without it, computed values like y (mean) and ymax (mean + SE) would not be accessible inside aes(). This pattern is essential whenever you want to show computed statistics — not raw values — on hover.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
4.5 Hover Effect with data_id Aesthetic
data_id is the second interactive feature of ggiraph. Elements sharing the same data_id value are highlighted together on hover:


Show code
latest <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

p <- ggplot(data = latest, aes(x = Young, y = Population, color = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(data_id = Continent),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Hover to highlight all countries in the same continent",
       x = "Young Population", y = "Total Population") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618
)






Interactivity: hovering over any point highlights all points sharing the same Continent value. The default hover CSS is fill:orange. Note that data_id and tooltip are independent: you can use either or both.






NoteWhat I Learned



data_id is the mechanism that makes ggiraph’s linking magic work. Assigning the same key to multiple elements (all points from Asia, for example) causes them to respond as a group. This is the foundation for coordinated views later.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
4.6 Styling the Hover Effect
CSS codes inside opts_hover() and opts_hover_inv() change the highlighting colour and dim non-hovered elements:


Show code
latest <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

p <- ggplot(data = latest, aes(x = Young, y = Population, color = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(data_id = Continent),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Styled hover: highlighted in dark, others dimmed",
       x = "Young Population", y = "Total Population") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618,
  options    = list(
    opts_hover(css     = "fill:#202020; stroke:#202020;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.15;")
  )
)






opts_hover() defines the CSS applied to the hovered element; opts_hover_inv() applies to all other elements. Setting opacity:0.15 on non-hovered elements creates a focus effect that naturally draws attention to the active group without removing context.






NoteWhat I Learned



The opts_hover_inv() pattern — dim everything else — is far more effective than just highlighting the active element. The contrast between opacity:0.15 (background) and the bold dark fill (hovered) creates immediate visual hierarchy with only two lines of CSS.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
4.7 Combining Tooltip and Hover Effect
Tooltip and data_id can be combined in the same aes() call:


Show code
latest <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(tooltip_text = paste0(
    "Country = ", Country,
    "\nContinent = ", Continent,
    "\nYoung = ", format(Young, big.mark = ","),
    "\nPopulation = ", format(Population, big.mark = ",")
  ))

p <- ggplot(data = latest, aes(x = Young, y = Population, color = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_text,
        data_id = Continent),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "turbo") +
  labs(title = "Combined: tooltip shows detail, hover highlights continent group",
       x = "Young Population", y = "Total Population") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618,
  options    = list(
    opts_hover(css     = "fill:#202020; stroke:#202020;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.15;")
  )
)






Interactivity: hovering over a point highlights the entire continent group and shows the individual country’s detail in the tooltip. The two aesthetics work simultaneously — tooltip gives individual context while data_id gives group context.






NoteWhat I Learned



Combining tooltip and data_id delivers two levels of information at once: the tooltip tells you what a specific point is, while the hover highlight tells you which group it belongs to. This layering is a powerful technique for hierarchical datasets like country-within-continent.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
4.8 Click Effect with onclick
The onclick aesthetic accepts any valid JavaScript string and executes it when a point is clicked:


Show code
latest_onclick <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(onclick_js = paste0(
    "window.open('https://www.google.com/search?q=",
    URLencode(paste(as.character(Country), "population statistics")),
    "')"
  ),
  tooltip_text = paste0(Country, "\n(click to search Google)"))

p <- ggplot(data = latest_onclick, aes(x = Young, y = Population, color = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_text,
        onclick  = onclick_js),
    size = 3.5, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Click any point to open a Google search for that country",
       x = "Young Population", y = "Total Population") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618,
  options    = list(
    opts_hover(css = "fill:#FF3333; cursor:pointer;")
  )
)












Warning



Note that click actions must be a string column in the dataset containing valid JavaScript instructions. The onclick value is evaluated as raw JavaScript in the browser — errors in the JS string will silently fail.


Interactivity: clicking a country point opens a new browser tab with a Google search for that country’s population statistics. sprintf() or paste0() with URLencode() are the standard approaches for building JavaScript window.open() strings safely.






NoteWhat I Learned



The onclick aesthetic turns every data point into a hyperlink — bridging the visualisation to external information systems. cursor:pointer in opts_hover() signals clickability to users without any additional UI elements. Always pre-compute the JS string as a data column; never build it inside aes().

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
4.9 Coordinated Multiple Views with ggiraph
When multiple ggiraph plots share the same data_id values and are combined with patchwork inside girafe(), hovering or clicking any element highlights its counterpart across all panels simultaneously:


Show code
latest <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(tooltip_text = paste0("<b>", Country, "</b><br>Continent: ", Continent))

p1 <- ggplot(data = latest, aes(x = Young, y = Population, color = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(data_id = Country,
        tooltip = paste0(tooltip_text,
                         "<br>Young: ", format(Young, big.mark = ","),
                         "<br>Population: ", format(Population, big.mark = ","))),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max(latest$Young, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05)) +
  labs(title = "Young vs Total Population", x = "Young", y = "Total Population") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11))

p2 <- ggplot(data = latest, aes(x = Young, y = Old, color = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(data_id = Country,
        tooltip = paste0(tooltip_text,
                         "<br>Young: ", format(Young, big.mark = ","),
                         "<br>Old: ", format(Old, big.mark = ","))),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "turbo") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max(latest$Young, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05)) +
  labs(title = "Young vs Old Population", x = "Young", y = "Old Population") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11))

girafe(
  code       = print(p1 + p2),
  width_svg  = 12,
  height_svg = 4.5,
  options    = list(
    opts_hover(css     = "fill:#202020; stroke:#202020;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
  )
)






Notice that when a data point in the left panel is hovered, the corresponding country point in the right panel is highlighted too. The data_id aesthetic is critical to link observations between plots — here Country is the shared key. The tooltip aesthetic is optional but provides useful per-point detail.
The programming strategy:

Appropriate interactive geoms with matching data_id values are used to create each panel.
patchwork (+) is used inside girafe(code = print(...)) — this is the key: passing combined plots through the code argument activates cross-panel linking.







ImportantWhat I Learned



Coordinated views require two things to work: (1) identical data_id keys across all panels, and (2) the patchwork combination placed inside girafe() via the code argument rather than ggobj. When both conditions are met, ggiraph handles all the JavaScript linking automatically — no manual event handling required.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
section: 5.1 Creating an Interactive Scatter Plot: plot_ly() Method
text:
5.1 Creating an Interactive Scatter Plot: plot_ly() Method
plot_ly() is plotly’s native interface. Column references use the tilde (~) notation:

The PlotThe Code Chunk











latest_plotly <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

plot_ly(data = latest_plotly,
        x    = ~Young,
        y    = ~Old)




The default output is already interactive: hover to see coordinates, drag to zoom, double-click to reset. The ~ before column names tells plotly to look up those names in the data argument. No additional configuration is required for a basic scatter.






NoteWhat I Learned



plot_ly() produces a fully interactive chart with a single call — no ggplot() + geom_*() chain required. The tilde syntax is distinctive to plotly and signals that the value is a column reference, not a literal. The default toolbar (zoom, pan, select, download) is added automatically.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
section: 5.2 Working with Visual Variables: plot_ly() Method
text:
5.2 Working with Visual Variables: plot_ly() Method
The color argument maps a qualitative variable to colour, and clicking legend symbols toggles trace visibility:

The PlotThe Code Chunk











latest_plotly <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

plot_ly(data   = latest_plotly,
        x      = ~Young,
        y      = ~Old,
        color  = ~Continent,
        colors = viridis::viridis(length(unique(latest_plotly$Continent))))




Interactivity: click on a continent’s colour symbol in the legend to show/hide that group. Double-click a legend item to isolate it. The colors argument accepts a named vector or a palette function — here viridis::viridis() generates the correct number of distinct colours automatically.






NoteWhat I Learned



Mapping color to a factor in plot_ly() automatically creates a legend with toggle interactivity — a feature that would require custom JavaScript in a static chart. Clicking continent symbols to isolate groups is far more intuitive for exploratory analysis than filtering data and re-rendering.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
section: 5.3 Creating an Interactive Scatter Plot: ggplotly() Method
text:
5.3 Creating an Interactive Scatter Plot: ggplotly() Method
ggplotly() converts any existing ggplot2 object into a plotly chart. The only extra line is the ggplotly() wrapper:

The PlotThe Code Chunk











latest_gg <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

p <- ggplot(data = latest_gg, aes(x = Young, y = Old, color = Continent)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max(latest_gg$Young, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05),
                  ylim = c(0, max(latest_gg$Old,   na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05)) +
  labs(title = "Young vs Old Population", x = "Young Population", y = "Old Population") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p)




Notice that the only additional line needed is ggplotly(p). The zoom toolbar, hover tooltips, and legend toggles are added automatically. This is the fastest path to interactivity when you already have a ggplot2 pipeline.






NoteWhat I Learned



ggplotly() is the bridge between the ggplot2 ecosystem and plotly interactivity. When you already have carefully crafted ggplot code (with proper themes, colour scales, labels), ggplotly() preserves all of it while adding the full plotly toolbar. The trade-off is that some advanced ggplot2 features (like geom_sf) may not translate perfectly.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
5.4 Coordinated Multiple Views with plotly
Creating coordinated linked plots with plotly involves three steps:

highlight_key() wraps the shared data object
Two scatter plots are built using ggplot2 functions on that shared data
subplot() places them side by side; clicking a point in one selects the corresponding point in the other


The PlotThe Code Chunk











latest_coord <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

d <- highlight_key(latest_coord)

p1 <- ggplot(data = d, aes(x = Young, y = Old)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7, color = "#831843") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max(latest_coord$Young, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05),
                  ylim = c(0, max(latest_coord$Old,   na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05)) +
  labs(title = "Young vs Old", x = "Young Population", y = "Old Population") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(data = d, aes(x = Young, y = Population)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7, color = "#1e40af") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max(latest_coord$Young,      na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05),
                  ylim = c(0, max(latest_coord$Population,  na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05)) +
  labs(title = "Young vs Total Population", x = "Young Population", y = "Total Population") +
  theme_minimal()

subplot(ggplotly(p1), ggplotly(p2))




Click on a data point in one scatter plot and see how the corresponding point in the other scatter plot is selected.
Key things to learn:

highlight_key() creates an object of class crosstalk::SharedData — both plots reference the same shared data object, which enables the selection to propagate across panels.
subplot() places plotly charts side by side and maintains the shared data linkage.
Visit the crosstalk documentation to learn more.







NoteWhat I Learned



highlight_key() is the glue between the two plots — it wraps a data frame with a crosstalk SharedData object so plotly knows which rows correspond across charts. Without it, subplot() would show two independent charts. The pattern is: shared data → two ggplots → two ggplotly calls → one subplot().

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
6.1 Interactive Data Table: DT Package
The DT package wraps the JavaScript DataTables library. R data frames rendered with DT::datatable() become searchable, sortable, and paginated HTML tables:

DT::datatable(pop_data, class = "compact")





The resulting table supports column sorting (click headers), global search (top-right box), and pagination. class = "compact" reduces row padding for denser display. No JavaScript knowledge is required.






NoteWhat I Learned



DT::datatable() is the fastest way to make any R data frame explorable in an HTML report. The search box alone — filtering 322+ rows in real time — replaces multiple manual filter() calls. Tables and charts are complementary: charts show patterns, tables expose individual values.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
6.2 Linked Brushing: crosstalk Method
Linked brushing connects a scatter plot and a data table so that selecting points in the plot filters the table rows, and vice versa:

The PlotThe Code Chunk





















latest_brush <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

d <- highlight_key(latest_brush)

p <- ggplot(d, aes(Young, Old, color = Continent)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max(latest_brush$Young, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05),
                  ylim = c(0, max(latest_brush$Old,   na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05)) +
  labs(x = "Young Population", y = "Old Population") +
  theme_minimal()

gg <- highlight(ggplotly(p), "plotly_selected")

crosstalk::bscols(gg,
                  DT::datatable(d),
                  widths = 5)




Click and drag to select points in the scatter plot — the table on the right will filter to show only those rows. Things to learn from the code:

highlight() is a plotly function that sets brushing options for linked selection. "plotly_selected" specifies that selection is triggered by a drag-select (lasso or box) in plotly.
bscols() is a crosstalk helper function that places HTML elements side by side using Bootstrap columns. It is designed for R Markdown and Quarto documents. Note: this brings in Bootstrap CSS.
The widths = 5 argument allocates 5/12 Bootstrap columns to each widget (plotly + DT), leaving 2 columns as margin.







ImportantWhat I Learned



Linked brushing with crosstalk combines two mental models: plotly handles the selection event (plotly_selected), highlight_key() propagates the selection to all widgets sharing the same SharedData object, and DT reacts by filtering its rows. The result is a powerful explore-and-drill-down workflow: spot a cluster in the chart, drag to select it, read the exact country names and values in the table — all without writing any JavaScript.

This exercise explores professional extensions to ggplot2 that enable publication-quality graphics. I’ll master label management, professional themes, and composite layouts while conducting deep statistical analysis of student performance patterns.

Learning Objectives:

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
2.1 Package Installation

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse, knitr, GGally, viridis, ggridges, 
               scales, gridExtra, reshape2)

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
2.2 Data Import

exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
4.1 Problem: Label Overlap Crisis


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#db2777") +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, se = TRUE, color = "#831843", fill = "#fbcfe8") +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), hjust = 0.5, vjust = -0.5, size = 2.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0,100), ylim = c(0,100)) +
  labs(title = "Label Overlap Problem: Unreadable IDs",
       x = "Mathematics Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()















WarningCritical Visualization Failure



With 322 data points, standard labeling creates complete overlap. The strong correlation (r = 0.831) is visible, but individual identification is impossible. This renders the visualization useless for student-level analysis.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
4.2 Solution: ggrepel Automatic Positioning


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 2, color = "#db2777") +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, se = TRUE, color = "#831843", fill = "#fbcfe8", size = 1.2) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), fontface = "bold", size = 2.5,
                   box.padding = 0.35, point.padding = 0.5, max.overlaps = 15) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0,100), ylim = c(0,100)) +
  labs(title = "ggrepel Solution: Perfect Readability",
       subtitle = paste0("All ", n_students, " students identifiable | r = ", round(cor_maths_english, 3)),
       x = "Mathematics Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()















TipIntelligent Positioning Algorithm



geom_label_repel() uses force-directed algorithms to: - Minimize label-label overlap - Maintain label-point connections - Optimize use of available space - Preserve data visualization integrity
Result: 100% readability with maintained correlation visualization.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
In this hands-on exercise, I explored the Grammar of Graphics using ggplot2 to create meaningful visualizations. Through analyzing exam data from Primary 3 students, I discovered the power of layered graphics and data-driven insights.
Let’s visualize data beautifully
Learning Objectives: - Master basic principles and essential components of ggplot2 - Plot statistical graphics based on the principle of Layered Grammar of Graphics - Conduct data-driven analysis with dynamic insights - Extract meaningful patterns from visualizations
